headline: Risk appetite improved into the close, but restrictive yields and only-partial breadth repair keep the tape constructive-cautious

volatility.name: VIX
volatility.sub: fear gauge fell back below the key 20 line

volatility.name: VVIX
volatility.sub: vol-of-vol stayed calmer than the prior spike

volatility.name: Put/Call
volatility.sub: call demand still outweighing put demand

volatility.name: SPX ATR(14)
volatility.sub: range compression improved, but not back to a quiet tape

rates.name: 2Y yield
rates.sub: front-end still high enough to keep the Fed on hold

fx.name: USD/JPY
fx.sub: still a live carry and BoJ-intervention watch

commodities.sub: precious metals still bid even as crude broke lower

decision.stance: Add only selectively; broad tape improved, but wait for better breadth confirmation before pressing risk
decision.keyLevels: SPX support sits around 7,360-7,400, with resistance around 7,450-7,520. Holding above the 50-day zone keeps the rebound constructive.
decision.invalidation: A renewed break below 7,360 with VIX back above 20-22, yields rising further, and credit softening would turn this back into correction defense.
watching: Can breadth get back above 50% of S&P 500 members over the 50-day?
Do 2Y and 10Y yields stop rising from the June 12 close?
Does oil stay under the mid-$80s to high-$80s zone, or rebuild a risk premium?
Do semis and growth hold leadership without the move narrowing again?
Does small-cap leadership keep broadening the tape next week?

agentCommentary: June 12 closed with risk appetite improving rather than accelerating. The S&P 500, Dow, Nasdaq, and Russell 2000 all finished higher, helped by a sharp drop in crude and a better preliminary University of Michigan sentiment reading, but the move still lacks full internal repair because only 46.5% of S&P 500 members sit above their 50-day average. Treasury yields stayed restrictive with the 2Y near 4.08% and the 10Y near 4.49%, so the tape remains constructive-cautious rather than clean risk-on. Credit is still calm, energy stress cooled materially, and the next test is whether next week's FOMC and post-oil price action let breadth catch up to price.
